coredata.dc:title: A fluorescence aptasensor based on GSH@GQDs and RGO for the detection of Glypican-3
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Huixue
Liu, Jinya
Chen, Wei
Na, Jintong
Huang, Yong
Li, Guiyin
coredata.dc:description: Glypican-3 (GPC3), a heparin sulfate proteoglycan, is a potential diagnostic and therapeutic target for hepatocellular carcinoma. In this paper, a novel fluorescent aptasensor for GPC3 detection is constructed via glutathione@graphene quantum dots–labeled GPC3 aptamer (GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt) as a fluorescence probe. First, GSH@GQDs is screened out with higher fluorescence intensity, which emits bright blue fluorescence under ultraviolet light. Then, the fluorescence-labeled GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt probe is formed by the combination of amination GPC3Apt and GSH@GQDs using EDC/NHS coupled reaction. Under hydrogen bond and π-π interaction/stacking, the fluorescence of GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt could be quenched by reductive graphene oxide (RGO) with the help of the photoinduced electron transfer and the fluorescence resonance energy transfer mechanism. In the presence of GPC3, the GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt specifically recognizes and binds to GPC3, giving rise to the change of secondary structure of GPC3Apt to form the GPC3/GPC3Apt-GSH@GQDs complex, which would lead to the disintegration of the GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt-RGO compound. Therefore, the energy transfer process is blocked and the fluorescence intensity is restored, enabling a highly sensitive response to GPC3. When the concentration of GPC3 is from 5.0 ng/mL to 150.0 ng/mL, the fluorescence recovery rate is well linearly related to GPC3 concentration with the limit of detection of 2.395 ng/mL (S/N = 3). This strategy shows recoveries from 98.31% to 101.89% in human serum samples and provides simple, fast and cheap analysis of GPC3, which suggests that it has great potential applications in clinical diagnosis for hepatocellular carcinoma.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Glypican-3
Glutathione@graphene quantum dots
Reductive graphene oxide
Fluorescence aptasensor

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Procedure of detection GPC3 with the fluorescence aptasensor
Detection of GPC3 in actual sample
Design and strategy for the fluorescence aptasensor based on GSH@GQDs and RGO
Spectroscopic and microscopic characterization of GSH@GQDs and RGO
Fluorescence quenching of GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt by RGO
Time dependence of the GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt-RGO system
Detection of GPC3 in standard aqueous solution
Determination of GPC3 in actual samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM images of GSH@GQDs; (B) Fluorescence emission spectrum and excitation spectrum of GSH@GQDs, the inset is the picture for GSH@GQDs under ultraviolet and natural light; (C) TEM images of RGO; (D) UV–vis spectra of RGO and fluorescence emission spectrum of GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt; (E) The particle size distribution of GSH@GQDs and RGO; (F) X-ray diffraction pattern of GSH@GQDs and RGO.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of GSH@GQDs and GSH@GQDs -GPC3Apt; (B) UV–vis spectra of GPC3Apt, GSH@GQDs and GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt; (C) The stability of GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt in different NaCl solution; (D) The stability of GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt in 10% BSA solution with different time.

(A) GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt fluorescence quenched by different concentrations of RGO; (B) The particle sizes changes of the different process for the constructed GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt-RGO-GPC3 system; (C) The surface Zeta potential changes of the different process for the constructed GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt-RGO-GPC3 system; (D) Time dependence of fluorescence quenching degree of GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt induced by RGO.
Condition optimization of (A) incubation time, (B) concentration of GSH@GQDs -GPC3Apt, (C) incubation temperature and (D) pH of PBS buffer.

(A) The fluorescence recovery of GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt-RGO to different concentrations of GPC3 from 5.0 ng/mL to 1200.0 ng/mL; (B) Calibration plot of fluorescence recovery rate ((F1-F0)/F0) against the GPC3 concentration; (C) Selectivity for possible interfering substances of the proposed fluorescent aptasensor.

(A) The principle of GPC3 detection based on GSH@GQDs -GPC3Apt-RGO system conjunction with specific binding of GPC3 aptamer to GPC3; (B) Fluorescence intensity recovery of GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt-RGO system with different concentrations of GPC3.
Comparison of the analytical parameters for a variety of methods for GPC3 detection.
Recovery assay for GPC3 in human serum samples (n = 3) by the proposed fluorescent aptasensor.

head.ce:title: A fluorescence aptasensor based on GSH@GQDs and RGO for the detection of Glypican-3

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Center for International Research of Bio-targeting Theranostics, Guangxi Key Laboratory of Bio-targeting Theranostics, Collaborative Innovation Center for Targeting Tumor Diagnosis and Therapy, Guangxi Talent Highland of Bio-targeting Theranostics, Guangxi Medical University, Nanning, Guangxi, 530021, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Center for International Research of Bio-targeting Theranostics
Guangxi Key Laboratory of Bio-targeting Theranostics
Collaborative Innovation Center for Targeting Tumor Diagnosis and Therapy
Guangxi Talent Highland of Bio-targeting Theranostics

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Center for International Research of Bio-targeting Theranostics, Guangxi Key Laboratory of Bio-targeting Theranostics, Collaborative Innovation Center for Targeting Tumor Diagnosis and Therapy, Guangxi Talent Highland of Bio-targeting Theranostics, Guangxi Medical University, Nanning, Guangxi, 530021, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life and Environmental Sciences, Guilin University of Electronic Technology, Guilin, Guangxi 541004, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Life and Environmental Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Life and Environmental Sciences, Guilin University of Electronic Technology, Guilin, Guangxi 541004, People’s Republic of China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence aptasensor for analyzing Glypican-3 (GPC3) based on GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt-RGO system has been developed. The GPC3 fluorescent aptasensor had an excellent analytical performance, convenient operation procedure and outstanding sensitivity, which could be applicable for detecting GPC3 level in human serum samples and thus can be used in the early diagnosis of HCC.

Glypican-3 (GPC3), a heparin sulfate proteoglycan, is a potential diagnostic and therapeutic target for hepatocellular carcinoma. In this paper, a novel fluorescent aptasensor for GPC3 detection is constructed via glutathione@graphene quantum dots–labeled GPC3 aptamer (GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt) as a fluorescence probe. First, GSH@GQDs is screened out with higher fluorescence intensity, which emits bright blue fluorescence under ultraviolet light. Then, the fluorescence-labeled GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt probe is formed by the combination of amination GPC3Apt and GSH@GQDs using EDC/NHS coupled reaction. Under hydrogen bond and π-π interaction/stacking, the fluorescence of GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt could be quenched by reductive graphene oxide (RGO) with the help of the photoinduced electron transfer and the fluorescence resonance energy transfer mechanism. In the presence of GPC3, the GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt specifically recognizes and binds to GPC3, giving rise to the change of secondary structure of GPC3Apt to form the GPC3/GPC3Apt-GSH@GQDs complex, which would lead to the disintegration of the GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt-RGO compound. Therefore, the energy transfer process is blocked and the fluorescence intensity is restored, enabling a highly sensitive response to GPC3. When the concentration of GPC3 is from 5.0 ng/mL to 150.0 ng/mL, the fluorescence recovery rate is well linearly related to GPC3 concentration with the limit of detection of 2.395 ng/mL (S/N = 3). This strategy shows recoveries from 98.31% to 101.89% in human serum samples and provides simple, fast and cheap analysis of GPC3, which suggests that it has great potential applications in clinical diagnosis for hepatocellular carcinoma.

ce:section.ce:para: Glypican-3 (GPC3) is a sulfate heparin-like proteoglycan involved in the regulation of ontogeny, cell growth and differentiation [1]. Scientific studies have shown that GPC3 is highly expressed or expressed in most Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC), while weakly expressed or not expressed in normal liver tissues and benign liver lesions. GPC3 has high sensitivity and specificity in the diagnosis of HCC, and the positive detection rate is higher than 50% [2]. At the same time, the expression level of GPC3 was also related to the prognosis of HCC [3]. Therefore, the highly sensitive detection of serum GPC3 content is of great significance for the early diagnosis and prognosis of HCC. Traditional methods of detection for GPC3 consist of liquid chromatography tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS), enzyme–linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), gas chromatography (GC), time-resolved fluorescence immunoassay (TRFIA) and immunohistochemistry (IHC) [4–6]. The determination of GPC3 by these methods has the characteristics of sensitivity, accuracy and high specificity. However, these methods require complex pretreatment process, professional operation, special instruments and expensive reagent [7]. Thus, there is a need to establish a rapid, inexpensive and portable GPC3 detection method.

Aptamer, known as “artificial substitution antibody”, is an artificially designed single-stranded oligonucleotide (ssDNA or RNA). Aptamer has the role of identifying target molecules (such as proteins, peptides, cells, viruses, enzymes, dye molecules, etc.) with tertiary structures (such as hair clips, pockets, bump rings, etc.) [8]. An aptamer for GPC3 (AP613-1) were selected in 2018 by Dong et al. [9] through capillary electrophoresis (CE)-SELEX technology and exhibited high specificity to GPC3, which binding affinities were in the nanomolar range (59.85 nM). And then, Alexa Fluor 750-conjugated AP613-1 is successfully used as a fluorescent probe to subcutaneous HCC imaging in xenograft nude mice. Recently, our group has constructed an electrochemical aptamer nanobiosensor based on hemin/graphene nanohybrids peroxidase-like catalytic silver deposition and GPC3 aptamer for the determination of GPC3 with the limit of detection of 3.16 μg/mL[10].

Until now, a large number of researches have designed biosensors based on aptamers for the detection of small molecule targets owing to their advantages of simple synthesis, low immunogenicity, and convenience of modification, and prominent specificity. These aptamer-based sensors (i.e aptasensor) include surface plasma resonance aptasensor [11,12], electrochemical aptasensor [13,14], fluorescence aptasensor [15] and so on. Among them, fluorescence aptasensor with high sensitivity, high selectivity and strong specificity has been developed to detect target molecules [16,17]. Fluorescence aptasensor is a kind of sensor that detects target molecules based on the change of fluorescence signal before and after the interaction between aptamer and target molecules [18]. For instance, Cai et al. [19] have developed a fluorimetric biosensor for probing trace cysteine (Cys) in hela cells and serum samples by using protein-encapsulated bimetallic gold-silver (Au-Ag) nanospheres combining with metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) of ZIF-8 acting as the robust fluorescent nano-probes. Fluorescence aptasensor are mostly involved in the quenching and recovery of fluorescence during signal transduction. Currently, there are four main sensing mechanisms for fluorescent aptasensor based on nanoparticles and aptamers: fluorescent resonance energy transfer (FRET) [20], internal filter effect (IFE) [21], photoinduced electron transfer (PET) [22] and aggregation induced quenching (ACQ) [23]. Among them, FRET has been widely applied as the important sensing mode based on the fluorescent quenching or enhancement mechanism. FRET is a non-radiative phenomenon with a pair of dipole-mediated energy transferred from an excited donor fluorophore to a proximal ground-state acceptor [24,25]. Li et al. designed a specificity and sensitivity fluorescent aptamer nanoprobe that used to detect alpha fetal protein (AFP) based on FRET between 5-Carboxyfluorescein (5-FAM) and palladium nanoparticles (PdNPs) [26]. Excellent donor–acceptor pair is a significant factor desired to improve the efficiency of FRET and the analytical performance. The most commonly used acceptor in FRET is graphene oxide (GO) [20,27] and reduced graphene oxide (RGO) [28,29]. RGO, a derivative of graphene, which is prepared by chemically reducing GO to decrease its oxygen content [30]. RGO is recently considered an attractive option due to its advantageous features in terms of cost-effective and bulk-scale productivity and versatility for chemical functionalization [31]. More importantly, compared with GO, RGO has a typical quasi-two-dimensional spatial structure and high fluorescence quenching efficiency in a wide wavelength range, which may be the reason that RGO has more π-π-conjugated structure and lower surface charge density [28,29]. Lu et al. [32] have systematically compared DNA adsorption and desorption from GO and RGO, found that under the same salt concentration, DNA adsorbs slightly faster with a 2.6-fold higher capacity on RGO. When its complementary DNA (cDNA) is added as a model target analyte, the RGO sample has a higher signal-to-background and signal to-noise ratio, while the GO sample has a slightly higher absolute signal increase and faster signaling kinetics. Wang Yan-Hong et al. [33] have found that the dosage of RGO needed for full quenching of the fluorescence (FAM-ssDNA) is 10 times lower than that of GO. The binding constant K values of RGO (0.17 mL/μg) is much larger than that of GO (1.6 × 10–6 mL/μg) toward FAM-ssDNA, which implied that the affinity of RGO with FAM-ssDNA was much stronger than that of GO with FAM-ssDNA. For instance, Wang et al. [29] have chosen RGO to effectively quench the fluorescence of the FAM-labeled probes to reduce the background signal and endowed the sensing strategy with a good performance for Pb2+ detection including low detection limit (0.17 nM), good selectivity, and satisfactory practical applicability. Therefore, we have chosen RGO as a fluorescence quencher in our study.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a new type of zero dimensional carbon nanomaterials, possess unique optical properties such as low toxicity, high surface to volume ratio, long luminous lifetime and difficulty in bleaching [34]. With these attractive advantages of GQDs, GQDs is regarded as a good fluorescent probe for biosensing and widely used as the donor of FRET sensor [20,28,29,35]. For instance, Bharathi G. et al. [36] have constructed a sensitive GQDs probe based on FRET which is detected for the HE4 biomarker. Tian et al. [37] found that the FRET between colloidal cerium oxide nanoparticles and GQDs could occur effectively by the electrostatic interaction, which could be interrupted/recovered controllably by the adding of ochratoxin A (OTA) aptamer and target OTA. The constructed ratiometric fluorescence aptasensor exhibited a satisfying linear range, low limit of detection (2.5 pg/mL) and good selectivity towards OTA.

Herein, we developed an aptamer-based fluorescent sensing platform to detect GPC3 based on the specific binding of GPC3 aptamer (GPC3Apt) to GPC3, and fluorescent quenching between glutathione@graphene quantum dots (GSH@GQDs) and RGO nanosheets. GSH@GQDs with high fluorescence efficiency was screened out and labelled GPC3Apt (denoted as GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt). RGO adsorbed and quenched the fluorescence of GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt based on the PET and FRET mechanism because of hydrogen bond and π-π interaction/stacking. When the target GPC3 is introduced into the GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt-RGO system, the GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt specifically recognizes and binds to the target GPC3, giving rise to the change of secondary structure of GPC3Apt to form the GPC3/GPC3Apt-GSH@GQDs complexes, which would quickly peel off the surface of RGO, hindering energy transfer process and resulting in fluorescence recovery. All of these could be applied for quantitative analysis of GPC3 in real sample. The analysis conditions include aptamer concentration, reaction temperature, reaction time and the pH value of PBS buffer are optimized, and outstanding analytical performance is accomplished. Additionally, the prepared fluorescent aptasensor is used to detect real human serum samples.

Glutathione, sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Company (Shanghai, China). Tris hydrochloride (Tris-HCI) and ascorbic acid (AA) were purchased from Xilong Scientific Company (Guangzhou, China). Graphene oxide (GO) was purchased from Nano Pioneer Company (Nanjing, China). Citric acid (C6H8O7·H2O) was purchased from Ascender Chemical Company (Shanghai, China). Amination GPC3 aptamer (GPC3Apt, 5′-TAA CGC TGA CCT TAG CTG CAT GGC TTT ACA TGT TCC A-NH2-3′) [38] was purchased from Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Phosphate buffered saline (PBS), bovine serum albumin (BSA), HEPES buffer, human serum albumin (HAS), immunoglobulin G (IgG) and immunoglobulin E (IgE) were purchased from Aladdin Reagents Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). All the chemicals and reagents used were of analytical grade and prepared with deionized water.

GSH@GQDs were obtained with the one-step method according to the improved synthesis method of Qu. et al [39]. Citric acid is used as a carbon source and glutathione is used a protective agent. Generally, adding 0.15 g glutathione to 0.5 g citric acid and mixing them, and putting them into a 10 mL beaker and heating to 260 °C in an electric heating jacket. The mixture was fully liquefied after reacting for about 5 min, the solution changed from colorless to light yellow, and then from yellow to brownish red within 20 min. Then dissolving the above solution in 50 mL ultra-pure water, taking dialysis treatment for 12 h. The obtained GSH@GQDs showed stable blue fluorescence and stored in the dark at 4 ℃ for further experiment.

The preparation method of reduced graphene oxide nanosheets is based on the method which is previously reported [40]. Typically, adding 5.0 mg GO into 50 mL ultra-pure water, and dispersing ultrasonically for 2 h to obtain 0.1 mg/mL GO solution. Subsequently, mixing 10.0 mg ascorbic acid and 10.0 mL GO solution together in a beaker of 50 mL, and stirring in a constant temperature agitator for 12 h. The RGO solution was obtained by centrifugating at 8000 rpm for 10 min.

10.0 mM EDC solution and NHS solution were mixed in a 4:1 ratio to form an EDC/NHS system, and 0.5 mL EDC/NHS solution was added into 5.0 mL GSH@GQDs for activated mixing [41]. 20.0 μL 180 nM amination GPC3 aptamer (GPC3Apt) were added to the activated GSH@GQDs solution (5.5 mL) and gently stirred in the dark for 2 h at room temperature. Conjugating the GSH@GQDs and GPC3 aptamer based on EDC/NHS coupled reaction. Then, separating the coupled GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt by centrifugating at 10000 rpm for 30 min. Subsequently, redispersing the residue in 1.0 mL ultra-pure water.

The conjugation between RGO and GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt was performed through hydrogen bond and π-π interaction. 20.0 μL of the above GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt solution and 20.0 μL 0.06 mg/mL of the RGO solution were added to 100.0 μL PBS solution (0.2 M, pH 7.0). Then stirring the solution gently for 1 h at 25℃. The fluorescence intensity of GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt-RGO under excitation at 330 nm was recorded with a fluorescent spectrophotometer. This conjugation exhibited weak fluorescence intensity (λex/em = 330/410 nm) (denoted as F0) because of FRET and PET mechanism between GSH@GQDs and RGO.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure of detection GPC3 with the fluorescence aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: 4.0 μL different concentrations of GPC3 solution was added into 66.0 μL conjugation GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt-RGO solution, incubated in an incubator at 25℃ for 60 min. Then, the fluorescence emission spectra of the resulting solution under excitation at 330 nm was collected for the fluorescence detection. With the addition of different concentrations of GPC3, the fluorescence intensity (λex/em = 330/410 nm) of the nanoprobe has been restored to varying degrees (denoted as F1). The degree of fluorescence recovery rate was calculated by the formal (F1-F0)/ F0.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of GPC3 in actual sample
ce:section.ce:para: The practicality of the fluorescence aptasensor was investigated in actual human serum samples through recovery experiments. We got the normal human serum samples from Guangxi Key Laboratory of Metabolic Disease Research, 924 Hospital of Chinese People's Liberation Army (Guilin, China). First, 2.0 μL of normal human serum was mixed with 2.0 μL GPC3 standard solutions of different known concentrations, which was used as the different spiked samples [42,43]. Then, the spiked samples (4.0 μL) were added into each well containing 66.0 μL of conjugation GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt-RGO solution. After incubating at 25℃ for 60 min, the fluorescence emission measurement (λex/em = 330/410 nm) is recorded in parallel three times. The fluorescence recovery rate was plotted against the regression equation of the calibration plot of the fluorescence aptasensor to determine the concentrations of the antigens and the recoveries were calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and strategy for the fluorescence aptasensor based on GSH@GQDs and RGO
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, a GPC3 fluorescence aptasensor was constructed based on the specific binding of GPC3 aptamer (GPC3Apt) to GPC3, and fluorescent quenching between GSH@GQDs and RGO, as shown in Scheme 1 A. The aptasensor includes two important parts of fluorescence quenching and fluorescence recovery in the detection process. GPC3Apt modified GSH@GQDs (GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt) was prepared with high fluorescence efficiency through EDC/NHS coupled reaction. GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt could be assembled on the RGO nanosheet surface through hydrogen bond and π-π interaction/stacking, which would cause the fluorescence energy transfer between GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt and RGO nanosheets, and quench the fluorescence of GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt based on the PET and FRET mechanism. In contrast, when the target GPC3 is added to the GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt-RGO system, GPC3 specifically recognizes and combines with GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt first based on the particular combination between the target molecule and the aptamer, giving rise to the change of secondary structure of GPC3Apt to form the GPC3/GPC3Apt-GSH@GQDs complex. Then, the GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt-GPC3 complex quickly peels off from the RGO surface, hindering the energy transfer process and resulting in fluorescence recovery. According to the degree of fluorescence recovery, a highly sensitive quantitative assay for GPC3 is realized conveniently. A working curve is then plotted based on the relationship between GPC3 concentration and fluorescence recovery intensity.

The feasibility of GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt-RGO system to detection GPC3 is conducted and the results are shown in Scheme 1B. GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt has the largest fluorescence intensity (1499) under λex/em = 330/410 nm (Scheme 1B, curve a). In the absence of GPC3 (i.e. GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt-RGO system (Scheme 1B, curve b), the FL intensity decreases to 855.5 due to RGO quenching the fluorescence of GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt. However, when the GPC3 is present (concentration, 0.001 mg/mL), the FL intensity restores to 925.3 (Scheme 1B, curve c). At the same time, there appears a large FL intensity response of 0.002 mg/mL GPC3 (Scheme 1B, curve d). Under the same reaction conditions, the addition of GPC3 to the GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt-RGO system weakens the interaction between GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt and RGO, because GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt prefers to bind GPC3 and form GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt-GPC3 complex. Therefore, GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt-GPC3 complex is separated from RGO, and the energy transfer process is inhibited, thereby restoring the FL intensity of GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt. The fluorescence intensity can be recovered to different degrees by adding GPC3 with different concentrations. Hence, the aptasensor shows that the positive analyte-dependent FL intensity for determining the target GPC3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectroscopic and microscopic characterization of GSH@GQDs and RGO
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, GSH@GQDs has been synthesized using citric acid as a carbon source and glutathione as a protective agent. The synthesized GSH@GQDs has strong fluorescence and good stability, which may be attributed to the amination reaction between the amine group of GSH and the carboxyl and epoxy groups on the surface of GQDs. Fig. 1 A shows the typical TEM image of GSH@GQDs. Obvious spherical dots can be seen in the TEM image, and the GSH@GQDs has a uniform particle size about 5–10 nm and good dispersion. The dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements reveals that the average particle size of the GSH@GQDs is near 66 ± 1 nm, which is larger than that of size measured by TEM due to its agglomeration [44] (Fig. 1E, curve b).

Fig. 1B is the fluorescence emission and excitation spectra of GSH@GQDs. As shown from Fig. 1B, the maximum emission peak of GSH@GQDs is at 440 nm (red line), and the maximum excitation peak is at 330 nm (blue line), respectively. In other words, the GSH@GQDs exhibited distinctly blue fluorescence with a peak maximum at 440 nm upon 330 nm excitation. In addition, the obtained GSH@GQDs emits bright blue fluorescence under ultraviolet, and light yellow under natural light (inset in Fig. 1B), further indicating the blue fluorescence properties of GSH@GQDs. GSH@GQDs emits fluorescence in a uniformly dispersed solution, which helps to improve the sensitivity of the biosensor.

Fig. 1C shows the typical TEM image of prepared RGO nanosheets. The lamellar black structure is well stratified with an average particle size of about 200 nm. The average size of RGO is about 201 ± 8 nm by the dynamic light scattering measurements (Fig. 1E, curve a). The UV–vis absorption spectra of RGO nanosheets in the ultraviolet region is strong while weak in the visible spectrum (Fig. 1D, curve a). Moreover, from Fig. 1D, though the RGO nanosheets has a maximum absorbance peak at 260 nm (Fig. 1D, curve a), the absorption graph from 300 nm to 600 nm overlaps with the fluorescence excitation of the GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt (Fig. 1D, curve b), which will confirm the occurrence of FRET phenomenon between RGO and GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt [20].

Also, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of as-prepared GSH@GQDs and RGO was shown in Fig. 1F. An abroad diffraction peak centered at 2θ = 24.63° for the GSH@GQDs attributes to turbostratic carbon phase similar to the graphite lattice spacing. Similarly, an abroad peak at 2θ = 25.04° correlates to the RGO, which is agreement with the reference [45].

The fluorescence probe GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt was obtained through the dehydration of the carboxyl group of GSH@GQDs and the amino group of GPC3Apt with EDC/NHS coupling. As shown in Fig. 2 A, GSH@GQDs and the GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt has different peak positions of characteristic fluorescence emission spectra. The maximum emission wavelength of the original GSH@GQDs is 440 nm under 330 nm excitation, while that of GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt is at 410 nm, which results in a blue shift phenomenon. This is mainly caused by the functionalization of GPC3Apt [46]. The fluorescence intensity of free GSH@GQDs was roughly 50% higher than that of GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt conjugate, indicating that GPC3Apt interacted with GSH@GQDs and quenched its fluorescence. This may be the reason that reaction between the carboxyl group of GSH@GQDs and the amino group of GPC3Apt occurred through EDC/NHS coupling and the assembly is induced by “π-π stacking” between GPC3Apt and GSH@GQDs, resulting in a decrease in fluorescence intensity. This result confirms that the GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt fluorescent probe is conjugated successfully.

Furthermore, UV–vis is used to verify the conjugation GSH@GQDs and GPC3Apt (Fig. 2B). The single GPC3Apt has the maximum absorption at 260 nm (Fig. 2B, curve a), while the GSH@GQDs and GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt have two maximum absorption peaks at 220 nm and 350 nm (Fig. 2B, curve b and curve c). The absorption peaks at 260 nm is very lower for GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt, which is attributed to the adsorption of GPC3Apt to the surface of GSH@GQDs and little amount of GPC3Apt. According to the changes of GPC3Apt content in the supernatant before and after the reaction, we can calculate the binding rate is 46.5%, which is almost consistent with the fluorescence result, indicating that GSH@GQDs and GPC3Apt were successfully combined.

The fluorescence intensity was measured in different concentrations of NaCl solution to investigate the stability of GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt. As shown in Fig. 2C, the fluorescence intensity is almost unchanged, which ruled out the influence of a high ionic strength environment on the stability of GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt. In order to confirm the stability of GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt in different pH environments, pH values were set to 6.0, 6.5, 7.0, 7.4 and 8.0 respectively, and the fluorescence intensity was measured. The fluorescence intensity is found to vary only slightly. Moreover, the GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt fluorescent probe was added into 10% BSA solution and placed away from light at 4℃ for 72 h. As shown in Fig. 2D, the fluorescence intensity of the probe is also found to be almost unchanged. This indicates that GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt has great development potential in biosensor applications under physiological conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching of GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt by RGO
ce:section.ce:para: It is well known that the ratio between the quencher molecules and the fluorescent probe has a great effect on the fluorescence assay. Thus, the working concentration of RGO was optimized to achieve the highest quenching efficiency. In brief, different concentrations of RGO in the range of 0.01 mg/mL to 0.06 mg/mL were prepared, 20.0 μL GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt solution (80 nM) and 20.0 μL RGO different concentrations of RGO were added into 100.0 μL PBS buffer, incubated for 1 h at room temperature with gentle stirring and measured the fluorescence intensity (λex/em = 330/410 nm). The fluorescence quenching degree of RGO is listed in Fig. 3 A. We can see from Fig. 3A, without RGO, GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt has the largest fluorescence intensity. When the concentration of RGO increases from 0.01 mg/mL to 0.06 mg/mL, the fluorescence intensity declines to various degrees. The fluorescence quenching degree of RGO on GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt increases continuously. When the concentration of RGO is 0.06 mg/mL, the fluorescence quenching degree of RGO on GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt is the maximum. Therefore, 0.06 mg/mL of RGO solution was chosen for further experiments.

In addition, the particle sizes and surface potential changes of the different process for the constructed GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt-RGO-GPC3 system were investigated through dynamic light scattering and Zeta potentials measurements (Fig. 3B and 3C). The particle size for GSH@GQDs and GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt are 66 ± 1 nm and 273 ± 6 nm. After the addition of RGO, the particle size of GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt-RGO is reduced to 144 ± 6 nm due to the strong absorption of GPC3Apt to the surface of RGO. The RGO adsorbed DNA tightly because it has lower surface negative charge and more aromatic regions for π − π stacking with DNA bases [32,33]. Therefore, the particle size of GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt-RGO is smaller than that of GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt. When GPC3 is present in the system, the particle size of GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt-RGO- GPC3 increases conspicuously (235 ± 2 nm), which is because GPC3 bonded with GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt, formed GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt-GPC3 complex, released from RGO surface.

Furthermore, Zeta potentials of RGO and GSH@GQDs is −8.55 ± 0.6 mV and −8.23 ± 0.6 mV, while the addition of GPC3Apt slightly increased the Zeta potentials (-7.22 ± 0.3 mV), which may be induced by the EDC/NHS coupled reaction between the carboxyl group of GSH@GQDs and the amino group of GPC3Apt. After the mixing of RGO and GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt, the adsorption effect resulted in a decrease in Zeta potentials (-14.77 ± 0.7 mV), suggesting the effect of photo induced electron transfer (PET) occurring during this process [22]. Subsequently, GPC3 is introduced into the mixture of GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt-RGO, Zeta potential declines to −16.0 ± 0.6 mV, which is due to the binding affinity between GPC3 and its corresponding GPC3Apt induced the complex from RGO surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Time dependence of the GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt-RGO system
ce:section.ce:para: The time dependence of the quenched fluorescence was studied for investigating the fluorescence stability of GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt-RGO system. Fig. 3D shows the FL intensity of FRET system. As time goes by, its fluorescence intensity gets lower and lower. After 40 min, the fluorescence intensity of GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt-RGO fluctuated little, indicating that the time required for fluorescence quenching equilibrium was about 40 min. Thus, 1 h incubation time for the conjugation of RGO with GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt was chose to ensure reaching the quenching equilibrium.

To make the constructed fluorescence biosensor have good sensing function, optimizing some factors of fluorescence recovery experiment is conducted. In the study, after introducing GPC3 in the GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt-RGO system, the fluorescence intensity was recorded as F1, and F0 represented the fluorescence intensity of GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt-RGO. Therefore, the fluorescence recovery rate is denoted as (F1-F0)/F0.

Firstly, the effect of the reaction time between GPC3 and GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt-RGO system was studied. As shown in Fig. 4 A, when the reaction time is between 10 min and 40 min, the fluorescence recovery rate increases, after 60 min, the fluctuation of fluorescence recovery rate is weak. Therefore, the optimal reaction time for fluorescence recovery experiment is 60 min.

Different concentrations of GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt resulted in different degrees of fluorescence recovery rate. The results are shown in Fig. 4B, the fluorescence recovery rate increases with the increasing of GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt up to 180 nM, and then decreases. In which 180 nM GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt has the highest of fluorescence recovery rate. Therefore, 180 nM of GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt is chosen for the fluorescence recovery experiments.

The effect of reaction temperature for GPC3Apt and GPC3 on the fluorescence of GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt-RGO-GPC3 was also studied. From Fig. 4C, when the temperature rises from 4℃ to 25℃, the fluorescence recovery rate of GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt-RGO-GPC3 system gradually rises, and then decreases from 25℃ to 50℃. Therefore, 25℃ is the optimal reaction temperature for fluorescence recovery.

The effect of pH of the PBS buffer on the fluorescence recovery rate was shown in Fig. 4D, the fluorescence recovery rate is different at different pH levels. When pH is 6.0, the fluorescence recovery rate is the highest. This can be explained by the fact that the aptamer is more stable at pH = 6.0 and not sufficiently stable when pH > 6.5, thereby reducing the interaction between the aptamer and GPC3. Thus, pH of 6.0 is the optimum pH in the subsequent experiments.

Through the above optimization experiments, the optimal reaction conditions is obtained as follows: reaction time of 60 min, reaction temperature of 25℃, pH of 6.0, and GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt concentration of 180 nM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of GPC3 in standard aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal reaction conditions, the dependence of fluorescence signal on the concentrations of GPC3 was further examined. As shown in Fig. 5 A, adding various concentrations of GPC3 (from 5 ng/mL to 1200 ng/mL) into the GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt-RGO system, the fluorescence intensity of the system is recovered, which indicates that the fluorescence recovery rate is dependent on the concentration of GPC3. As shown in Fig. 5B, there is a linear relationship between fluorescence recovery rate and concentration of GPC3 when it ranges from 5 ng/mL to 150 ng/mL. The linear regression equation is Y = 0.02979 + 7.88742E-4*X (where Y is the fluorescence recovery rate and X is the concentration of GPC3), and the correlation coefficient is 0.99599. The detection limit (LOD) was 2.395 ng/mL (S/N = 3). In addition, after detecting 100 ng/mL GPC3 in five replicates, and the RSD was 4.52%, which obtained the good repeatability of this GPC3 detection method.

In addition, many methods for detecting GPC3 have been reported before, we compared the fluorescence assay with them. From Table 1 , the proposed GPC3 fluorescence aptasensor has a wide linear range with higher sensitivity. First of all, the GPC3 aptamer, can bind to GPC3 protein with high specificity and affinity [38]. Then, compared with electrochemical aptasensor for detecting GPC3 [10,40,42], the fluorescence aptasensor applied in this experiment demonstrated low cost manufacturing process, short analysis time and high sensitivity. Although, LOD of the GPC3 aptasensor is slightly higher than ELSIA immunoassay [47,50] and other immunosensor [42,48]. Aptamer, used as recognition molecule in the study, has good stability, easy accessibility, affordable prices, and minimal immune response in comparison to antibodies [49]. Thus, the GPC3 fluorescence aptasensor has acceptable selectivity, great sensitivity and reproducibility, lower cost and shorter time in the detection range of 5.0–150 ng/mL, which has the potential to solve the problem of early clinical diagnosis of HCC.

To make the constructed fluorescence sensor have good function, the selectivity of the GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt-RGO sensor was tested by using some interference substance (such as BSA, HAS, IgG, IgE) or mixing the interference substance with GPC3 at the same concentration (1.0 μg/mL) for fluorescence recovery experiment under the same experimental procedures. As shown in Fig. 5C, the fluorescence recovery rate for GPC3 detection was much higher than that of other interferers, so it could be concluded that GSH@GQDS-GPC3Apt was specific to GPC3. The fluorescence recovery rate obtained by the mixture was close to that obtained by GPC3 alone, indicating that the prepared GPC3 fluorescence aptasensor has an excellent selectivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of GPC3 in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to determine the availability of the fluorescence aptasensor, we carried out the recovery experiment of GPC3 in the spiked human serum samples under optimized experimental condition [25]. In this test, three spiked normal human serum samples which included various concentrations of GPC3 (20.0 ng/mL, 40.0 ng/mL, 80.0 ng/mL) were obtained [42,43]. The spiked serum was added as target analyte respectively into the GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt-RGO system and analyzed using fluorescence analysis. The fluorescence recovery rate was plotted against the regression equation of the calibration plot of the fluorescence aptasensor to determine the concentrations of GPC3 and recoveries were calculated. Analytical results were shown in Table 2 , the recoveries of GPC3 are 98.31%-101.89% with RSDs of 0.56–1.23%. The results indicates that the aptasensor has good reproducibility and can detect GPC3 in human serum samples with reliable accuracy, which implied a good potential for GPC3 quantification in the practical application.

In conclusion, we have successfully developed a novel fluorescence sensing platform for GPC3 based on interaction between GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt and RGO. The FL intensity of GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt quenched by RGO based on the photoinduced electron transfer and the fluorescence resonance energy transfer mechanism. And FL intensity was restored after the addition of GPC3 to the GSH@GQDs-GPC3Apt-RGO system due to the binding of GPC3 and GPC3Apt, thereby restoring GSH@GQDs fluorescence. There is a linear relationship between fluorescence recovery rate and concentration of GPC3 when it ranges from 5 ng/mL to 150 ng/mL. The LOD of the fluorescence aptasensor is 2.395 ng/mL. Moreover, this detection method has been successfully applied to the detection of human serum samples with good recovery rate (98.31%-101.89%). The novel fluorescence aptasensor can rapidly detect GPC3 with simple, portable, and sensitive performance, indicating that this proposed method has a good potential application for detection of GPC3 in biochemical sensing.
The human serum samples used in this study were approved by the Guangxi Key Laboratory of Metabolic Diseases Research Ethics Committee in Guilin, China.

Huixue Wang: Software, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Jinya Liu: Visualization, Investigation, Data curation. Wei Chen: Supervision, Data curation. Jintong Na: Software, Validation. Yong Huang: Writing – review & editing. Guiyin Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.81760534), the Open Fund of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Bio-targeting Theranostics (Nos. GXSWBX201902 and GXSWBX201903), the State Project for Essential Drug Research and Development (No. 2019ZX09301132).
